turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: The second case is Food Marketing Institute v. Argus Leader and is quite different. This one concerns the Freedom of Information Act, also known as FOIA. FOIA instructs federal agencies to make their records available to the public upon request, but it also contains a number of exceptions, detailing categories of information that the agency may withhold. Today we consider Exemption 4, which shields from disclosure commercial or financial information provided by private parties to the government if that information is also confidential. This case presents the seemingly simple question, when does information provided to a federal agency qualify as confidential? This particular dispute started when a South Dakota newspaper filed a FOIA request for data collected by the Department of Agriculture in connection with the National Food Stamp Program known as a Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program or SNAP. The paper asked for, among another things, data reflecting the amount each retail store claimed from the agency each year for food it sold through the SNAP program. The US Department of Agriculture withheld this store level SNAP data relying for the purposes of this case on Exemption 4. Retailers customarily do not disclose their store level sales data publicly because it can be used by rivals to secure a competitive advantage. Recognizing this and seeking to induce retailers to participate in SNAP and provide store level information to the government that it needs for its own programs, the government has long promised participating retailers that it will keep their information private. By every indication the government was correct to apply Exemption 4. Common usage, contemporary dictionaries and statutory context suggest two conditions that might be required for information to be considered confidential under FOIA. First, information is often considered confidential when it is customarily kept private or at least closely held by the person in parting it. Second, information might be considered confidential only if the party who receives it provides some assurance that he or she will keep it secret. In this case we do not have to decide whether only the first or perhaps both of those conditions are essential to trigger Exemption 4, because the store level SNAP data at issue here clearly satisfied both conditions. The retailers kept it private and the government promised to keep it private. The Court of Appeals erred in holding otherwise. Following a test originally developed decades ago by the DC Circuit in National Parks and Conservation Association v. Morton, the Eighth Circuit in this case held that information qualifies as confidential only if it is likely to cause substantial harm to the competitive position of the person from whom the information was obtained if it is disclosed. And while the Court found that retailers would be harmed by the disclosure of their SNAP data, it could not be sure it said that the harm would be substantial. Today we reject National Parks' substantial competitive harm test as a relic from a bygone era of statutory construction. That test finds no support in the statutory text and no support in the ordinary public meaning of the word confidential. In fact, National Parks did not even attempt to grapple with the statute's text or ordinary public meaning, but instead to support its test relied heavily on statements from witnesses in a congressional hearing years earlier on a different piece of legislation that was never enacted into law, and that is not so much a legitimate judicial interpretation of a statute as it is the illegitimate judicial rewriting or writing of a new one. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals. Justice Breyer has filed an opinion concurring in part and dissenting in part, in which Justices Ginsburg and Sotomayor have joined.
